quick_facts.label: Official occupational classification
quick_facts.value: India’s National Classification of Occupations (NCO 2015) lists “Manager, Nursery” under code 1311.0900 in the Agricultural and Forestry Production Managers group.

quick_facts.label: Plant-nursery scope
quick_facts.value: For this role, “nursery” means a commercial horticultural plant nursery producing trees, shrubs, flowers, ornamental plants or vegetables for trade or retail customers.

quick_facts.label: Relevant higher-education foundations
quick_facts.value: ICAR’s undergraduate curricula include plant propagation and nursery management, pest and disease management, soil fertility and nutrient management, economics and marketing in horticulture; forestry curricula include tree seed and nursery technology, forest health and protection, forest economics and marketing, and forest business management.

quick_facts.label: Skills-training context
quick_facts.value: As listed by the Agriculture Skill Council of India on July 18, 2026, relevant NSQF-aligned qualifications include Plant Nursery Associate (Level 3), Gardener cum Nursery Raiser (Level 4), and Professional Gardening and Nursery Management (Level 4.5). Qualification availability and validity dates can change.

sections.heading: What a Nursery Manager does
paragraphs: A Nursery Manager plans what plant material to raise and in what quantities, taking account of demand, growing conditions and technical requirements such as germination, plant growth habits, soils, nutrients and disease control. The role also includes selecting inputs such as seed, nutrients and plant-protection materials.

The manager monitors crop and work quality; directs nursery workers undertaking sowing, raising, plant-growth and disease-control activities, and transplanting, potting or cutting plants for sale. Commercial responsibilities extend to records, accounts, marketing and, where applicable, purchasing stock for resale and selling related garden inputs.
bullets: Plan production mix and volumes for commercial plant sales.
Manage propagation, crop health, growing media, nutrition and plant handling.
Supervise workers and inspect nursery crops, soil and work quality.
Maintain operational, inventory, accounting and marketing records.

sections.heading: Entry routes in India
paragraphs: A strong formal route is B.Sc. (Hons.) Horticulture. ICAR’s model undergraduate curriculum includes practical-credit courses in plant propagation and nursery management for fruit, plantation, vegetable, flower and medicinal crops, alongside protected cultivation, pest and disease management, nutrient management, economics and marketing, and an experiential-learning or internship component.

B.Sc. (Hons.) Forestry is also a relevant route, particularly for tree, forest or plantation nurseries: the ICAR curriculum includes Tree Seed and Nursery Technology, Forest Health and Protection, Forest Economics and Marketing, Forest Business Management, and a commercial tree-seedling-production elective.

A work-based route can begin with a nursery-raising or plant-nursery associate qualification and supervised nursery experience, then build toward broader nursery-management capability. ASCI lists occupational standards in these areas; candidates should verify the current qualification version, approved training provider and validity before enrolling.

The graph parent “Nursery Teacher” should not be treated as a like-for-like preparation route for this plant role. This is an inference from the official NCO definition, which explicitly concerns horticultural plants and commercial nursery operations rather than early-years education.
bullets: B.Sc. (Hons.) Horticulture: best-aligned for fruit, vegetable, floriculture, ornamental and protected-cultivation nurseries.
B.Sc. (Hons.) Forestry: especially relevant for tree-seedling, agroforestry, plantation and forest nurseries.
NSQF-aligned training plus hands-on nursery work: a practical pathway into junior nursery operations before management responsibility.

sections.heading: Core skills to develop
paragraphs: Technical capability should cover propagation, plant germination and growth, soils and growing media, nutrient management, irrigation, pest and disease management, and crop inspection. These capabilities follow directly from the NCO responsibilities and are reinforced by ICAR horticulture and forestry curriculum content.

Management capability matters equally: a manager needs to schedule and supervise nursery work, procure inputs, maintain production and business records, interpret market needs, and support sales and marketing. Communication and practical worker supervision are therefore central, not optional, skills.
bullets: Plant propagation and nursery production practices.
Plant-health observation, pest and disease management, and hygiene.
Soil, media, nutrition and irrigation management.
Quality control for planting material and crop handling.
Labour supervision, procurement, inventory and record keeping.
Demand awareness, marketing and customer-facing sales operations.

sections.heading: Workplace and operating context
paragraphs: The work is based in commercial horticultural nurseries serving trade or retail buyers. Depending on the crop and business model, production may occur in controlled environments such as hothouses or greenhouses, or in field conditions under natural weather.

The role combines plant-production oversight with operational work: inspecting crops and work areas, supervising nursery staff, managing inputs and records, and coordinating marketing activities. The balance between production, retail and administrative work will vary by nursery type and scale.
bullets: Field nursery beds and outdoor growing areas.
Protected structures such as greenhouses or hothouses.
Stock, input, record-keeping and customer-sales operations.
